pull.interruption_note: First pull process was killed (SIGKILL) at ~80% completion (~14GB of 17GB blob) due to subagent exec budget exhaustion. Process was re-launched as a true detached background process (nohup). The Ollama pull command is idempotent and resumed from the partial blob.

warmup.request: single-token minimal completion to force model load
warmup.interpretation: Model loaded fully on GPU (100%). RSS of host process barely changed because the actual model weights live in GPU memory, not in the ollama orchestrator process. Memory pressure 96% pre-pull, still healthy post-load.

runs.note: Includes 268ms model-in-context cost; first run after warmup completion.

benchmark_2_verify_prompt.description: ~431 token CREST Verify verdict prompt, num_predict=200, repeated 2 times

runs.note: First long-prompt eval (cold cache for this prompt).
Prompt cache fully hot (eval latency collapses to 81ms).

benchmark_3_long_context.description: Single call with ~6764 prompt tokens (est ~8266 from 33k char input) and num_predict=150
benchmark_3_long_context.observation: Ollama tokenizer reports 6764 tokens for 33k chars (approx 4.9 chars/token, denser than my 4 chars/token heuristic). Either way, well above 8k target.

run.model_ps_during: gemma4:26b 17 GB 100% GPU 262144 until 4 min from now

benchmark_3_long_context.interpretation: Long context handled cleanly. 6764-token prompt took 13.3s to process (single forward pass over KV cache), then 150 tokens at 51.8 t/s. The model comfortably fits 6764 tokens in its 262144 context window (2.58% utilization). Memory pressure stable, zero swap activity. The 51.8 t/s vs 57.4 t/s drop suggests some KV-cache attention overhead at larger contexts, but well within usable range.

concurrent_load.description: Two simultaneous short requests against single model instance

request_a.prompt: What is the capital of France? Answer in one word.

concurrent_load.interpretation: Both concurrent requests returned 200 OK with no errors, no timeouts, no slowdown. Ollama serializes at the model level (single 26B weights on single M4 Pro 16-core GPU) but request B (queued behind A) completed in 1.38s vs A's 2.45s because A was already partially through its eval when B started. Per-request throughput held at ~58 t/s under 2x load, indicating the GPU and host have headroom for concurrent inference workloads. Worth noting this is the same model in a single Ollama instance; running two model replicas in parallel would compete for the same ~17GB of GPU memory.

unload_recovery.description: Natural unload after idle timeout (4 minutes default)
unload_recovery.interpretation: Ollama's 4-minute idle unload timer is well-behaved. Memory is returned to the system cleanly: pages free jumped from 22k to 1.6M (~25.9 GB reclaimed), and the orchestrator process stays resident at ~128MB to handle future API requests. No swap I/O observed. Unload is non-disruptive: a subsequent /api/generate call will reload the model in ~5.4s.

system_capacity_assessment.system_memory_free_pct_post_load: ~95 (4.6k pages free of 3.1M = 0.15% pages, but only ~1.7GB active wired)
system_capacity_assessment.model_size_in_gpu_memory_gb_estimate: ~17 (matches disk; quantized weights still load into unified memory)
system_capacity_assessment.post_unload_state: clean, no swap, no leaked state
failure_modes_observed: Long-running pull commands can be killed by exec-timeout budgets in non-interactive shells; solution is to background the pull with nohup and poll model list until registered.

comparison_context.speed_drop_short_prompt_pct: n/a (no 8b baseline measured in this test)

risks_observed.finding: Ollama 4-minute unload timer means an idle model sits in GPU memory for 4 minutes after last use. On a 48GB M4 Pro this is fine (17GB model uses ~35% of unified memory), but if multiple large models are kept warm simultaneously, pressure would build.

Tokens/sec drops from 57.4 (short prompt) to 51.8 (6.7k token context) due to attention overhead. This is a normal scaling pattern. For typical Yoda/CREST Verify prompts (1-4k tokens), expect ~55-57 t/s.
Default Ollama idle unload is 4 minutes. For latency-sensitive workflows, consider preloading via a no-op /api/generate on a cron or keepalive parameter to keep the model warm.
26B model requires 17GB on disk. With gemma4:8b (9.6GB) already present, total OC2A Ollama footprint is 26.6GB. Still well within the 460GB disk. Even gemma4:31b (estimate ~20GB) would fit alongside.

Quantization is Q4_K_M (medium quality, ~4 bits per weight). This is the standard 'good enough' tradeoff for local inference. For higher quality at the cost of size, Q8_0 or f16 quantizations exist but would roughly double or quadruple the size.

recommendation_rationale: OC2A can reliably host gemma4:26b locally. The model pulls in ~9 minutes at sustained 24 MB/s, fits in 48GB unified memory with substantial headroom, loads in 5.4s on first call and ~250ms on warm calls, sustains 56-57 tokens/sec for typical Yoda/CREST prompts, handles 6.7k-token contexts with only modest slowdown, and supports concurrent inference with no failures. Memory is cleanly reclaimed on idle unload. The only operational concern is the 4-minute idle timer, which is configurable if needed. For local LLM workloads in production, gemma4:26b is a strong fit. The 31B variant was not tested in this run but is a reasonable next step if Ken wants to push quality higher; based on this 26B test, it should also fit (~20GB estimated) with a 20-30% expected speed reduction.
go_with_caveats_notes: If 31B is also wanted, expect ~20GB disk and ~40-45 t/s.
If sub-200ms first-token latency is required, preload the model on a schedule.
Consider monitoring Ollama process RSS over time; orchestrator is ~128MB and stable.
next_steps_recommended: CHG-0953: Mark test as passed. Update TKT-1014 metadata (test_completed_at, report_path, recommendation=GO, key_metrics).
If desired, run a second capacity test for gemma4:31b to validate headroom assumptions before promoting 31B to production.
Consider adding `OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE` configuration in OpenClaw gateway if 4-min unload timer causes cold-start issues in production traffic.
Document gemma4:26b in the model registry / model-routing skill so future routing decisions can consider it as a local option for non-ultra-low-latency workloads.
